title: New homeownership measure puts people first | Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis
excerpt: An alternative way of calculating the homeownership rate tells us how many individuals own their homes—something the most commonly used metric fails to track
content_html:
The homeownership rate in the United States is reported to be 65 percent. But this commonly cited data point on homeownership is actually the owner-occupancy rate, which tells us how many housing units are occupied by an owner. While owner occupancy is an interesting measure, it doesn’t tell us how many people own their home. As an alternative to better reflect the share of adults who are homeowners, we offer the homeowners-to-population ratio, or HPOP, a measure that lends a more nuanced view for important policy considerations and context. Using this new measure, the U.S. homeownership rate is 53 percent.

To further its mission of pursuing a growing economy and stable financial system that work for all of us, the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis works to understand economic conditions in the Ninth Federal Reserve District and beyond. Homeownership trends are a critical component of those conditions. Housing is both the largest expense and the largest source of wealth for many families. Using the HPOP helps us understand housing better in several ways. For example, the HPOP accounts for millions of American adults who would not be identified as either homeowners or renters under the traditional owner-occupancy measure. By including all adults, the HPOP gives us a more accurate understanding of the economic well-being of Americans. Measuring homeownership by the person instead of by the home is particularly important for comparing characteristics of homeowners: by age, by geography, and across time.

Understanding homeownership measures

To better understand the difference between the traditional owner-occupancy measure and the HPOP, consider a hypothetical cul-de-sac with five housing units, each of which is home to a separate group of residents:

Housing unit 1. A couple owns their home. The woman’s parents live with them.
Housing unit 2. A couple owns their home. Their son, a recent college graduate, lives with them.
Housing unit 3. A man owns his home. A friend lives with him.
Housing unit 4. A couple owns their home. Their two young children live with them.
Housing unit 5. Three roommates rent their home. The owner lives elsewhere.

As illustrated in Figure 1, these five housing units are home to 14 adults. Because four of these five housing units have their respective owners as residents, the owner-occupancy rate—the measure traditionally viewed as the homeownership rate—on the cul-de-sac is 80 percent. However, because only seven of the 14 adults are actually owners of the homes they live in, the HPOP is much lower, at 50 percent.

Calculating the HPOP

To calculate the HPOP, we divide the total population of adult homeowners by the total adult population. For this analysis, we use data from the U.S. Census Bureau’s American Community Survey (ACS).1 To determine the total population of adults, we exclude anyone under age 18. To identify homeowners, we then look among the adults living in owner-occupied housing. The ACS tags the homeowner within such homes as the “reference person” (or “head”) for their household. We then also include any spouses or unmarried partners of the reference person as homeowners.2

The HPOP categorizes adults who are not homeowners more precisely than the owner-occupancy rate. The largest group of non-homeowners, renters, is accounted for in both measures. However, calculations of the owner-occupancy rate leave out people who are living in group quarters—such as college dormitories, nursing homes, and correctional facilities. Our calculations of the HPOP include those groups and also account for discrepancies in the number of adults in renter households relative to homeowner households. For instance, if 50 out of 100 homes were rented by 50 adults living alone and the other 50 homes were owned and occupied by 100 adults living as married couples, then the owner-occupancy rate would be 50 percent but the HPOP would be 67 percent.

Our approach also quantifies that 13.9 percent of adults in the United States live in owner-occupied homes but are not owners themselves. In other words, more than one in eight of the nation’s adults are misrepresented in the most-cited statistic on homeownership. As Figure 2 indicates, these adults include adult children living with their homeowning parents; older parents living with their homeowning adult children; other relatives, such as siblings or cousins; and other unrelated adults, such as friends or roommates.

Loading chart 2...

New measure highlights reductions in numbers of young homeowners

Policymakers are often interested in homeownership rates for specific population groups. Homeownership’s role in wealth building and financial independence has led to interest in the Gen Z and Millennial generations’ homeownership rates. For example, one recent survey garnered a lot of attention when it put the median age of a first-time home buyer at 40 years old (though several other data sources suggest the median first-time home buyer’s age is actually in the early 30s).

The HPOP categorizes adults who are not homeowners more precisely than the owner-occupancy rate.

Since the owner-occupancy rate is defined at the housing-unit level, descriptions of owner-occupancy rates by demographic characteristics, such as age, race, or sex, can be misleading. In the owner-occupancy approach, one homeowner or renter is defined as the head of a household. That person’s demographic characteristics are then used to represent all the residents. An advantage of the HPOP is that since it’s defined using all the adult residents, it can more accurately describe homeownership by demographic characteristic. These differences become clear when we look at homeownership rates by age.

Across both the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP, young people have low homeownership rates. Even so, the owner-occupancy rate inflates homeownership among young adults, in two ways. First, as noted above, owner-occupancy rates by age are defined by the age of the household head. Because only a third of adults under age 35 are household heads,3 many young adults are not reflected in the rate calculation. This includes students or young workers who choose to reside at home with their parents until they’ve saved up some money or increased their earnings. Second, the owner-occupancy approach fails to account for anyone living in college dorms. They’re excluded from the calculation because dorms are categorized as group quarters.

Since the HPOP is defined using all adult residents, it can more accurately describe homeownership by demographic characteristic.

The owner-occupancy rate for households headed by adults under age 35 was 37 percent in 2024. Using the HPOP, which accounts for co-residents and dorm dwellers, we find a substantially lower number: only 22 percent of adults under 35 own their homes.

Switching from the owner-occupancy rate to the HPOP also highlights how homeownership rates by age have evolved over time. Our HPOP approach indicates that the data from the past two decades have at least two stories to tell. First, young people’s homeownership rate fell and then rose. From 2006 until 2015, the HPOP among younger Americans dropped. For example, the HPOP for a 25-year-old fell from 20 percent in 2006 to 12 percent in 2015. It then increased from 2015 to 2024. As shown in Figure 3, in 2024 14 percent of 25-year-olds owned their homes. Second, the HPOP among adults over age 70 climbed upward during both periods, increasing by 5 percentage points in total. This could be the result of increased longevity and financial security among this group. In contrast, if we use owner-occupancy as our measure, we find only a 1 percentage point increase—a finding that largely misses the homeownership rate trend among older adults over this period. One reason the findings are different might be that many older adults would have either been co-residing with their children or living in nursing homes and thus not captured by the owner-occupancy measure.

Loading chart 3...

Implications at the state level

In addition to shedding light on differences in homeownership by demographics, the HPOP paints a significantly different picture of how homeownership varies among states. In doing so, it presents a stronger relationship between housing prices and homeownership than the owner-occupancy rate does. Our analysis shows that every state has a lower HPOP than owner-occupancy rate. In Figure 4, the y-axis values of the gold dots represent the differences between the two measures for all 50 states. We find that states with higher housing costs tend to have even lower HPOPs relative to their owner-occupancy rates. To measure housing prices, we use the rent-to-income ratio, which is the average annual rent divided by the average personal income among adult renters. Rent is generally a more consistent measure of housing prices, since the typical cost of owning a home can vary as mortgage rates and other homeownership costs shift over time. Comparing rents to incomes allows us to factor in state-by-state differences in typical wages. The higher the rent-to-income ratio, the higher a state’s housing costs.

Loading chart 4...

Why would the difference between the two homeownership measures be so closely tied to housing costs? The most obvious explanation is that in places where housing is cheaper, more adults are buying their own homes. Of course, monthly expenses are only one factor people consider when they are deciding how—and with whom—they’d like to live. Housing situations might change when people get married. Some adults might want to take care of their aging parents or give their adult children a more affordable place to live. Some homeowners might prefer living with other people, leading them to rent out a spare room instead of using it as a guest room or an office.

As higher housing costs motivate more adults to live together, the difference between the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP grows.

Personal preferences influence some of these decisions. But economic conditions, such as the price of housing, are another influence. For example, the more expensive it is to rent or own a home, the more appealing it is to split those costs with others. As higher housing costs motivate more adults to live together, the difference between the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP grows.

Figure 5 allows users to see precisely how large the difference is between the two homeownership measures for each state. It also displays the ranking change that occurs when shifting from the owner-occupancy rate to the HPOP. 

5

To continue our examination of housing costs and homeownership, consider the implications for two states: Hawaii and North Dakota. Hawaii, which appears as the topmost gold circle back in Figure 4, is nearly the least affordable state, as measured by its rent-to-income ratio of 0.57. Consistent with the idea that higher housing costs might discourage adults from forming their own households, Hawaii’s HPOP is 18.9 percentage points lower than its owner-occupancy rate, the largest difference of any state.

On the other hand, North Dakota—one of four states that lie wholly within the Ninth Federal Reserve District, which the Minneapolis Fed serves—is the most affordable state in the nation by rent-to-income ratio. As we might expect, it has the smallest difference between its owner-occupancy rate and its HPOP. This small difference reflects, for example, how few adults under age 35 co-reside with parents in North Dakota. Only 10 percent of North Dakota’s young adults live with a parent, compared to the national average of 30 percent. While the gap between the two homeownership measures is more than 10 percentage points for the majority of states, in North Dakota the difference is just 3.9 percentage points. As a result, North Dakota’s homeownership ranking among the 50 states rises from forty-seventh to twenty-fourth when using the HPOP instead of the owner-occupancy rate.

Minnesota, South Dakota, and Montana—the other three states that lie wholly within the Ninth District—are relatively affordable places, as measured by rent-to-income ratio, and their homeownership rankings look more like North Dakota’s than Hawaii’s. As we might expect based on the relationship between housing costs and homeownership-measure gaps, their HPOPs are modestly lower than their owner-occupancy rates, with differences of 8.9, 6.0, and 10.1 percentage points, respectively, compared with a nationwide difference of 12.2 percentage points. Despite having higher housing costs, Montana ranks eighteenth in both the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP. Figure 5 shows that both Minnesota and South Dakota improve their homeownership rank when we use the HPOP as the measure, moving from eighth and twentieth place, respectively, to fifth and seventh place.

Implications for public policy

Using the owner-occupancy rate as a measure of homeownership can still serve an important purpose. For example, in cases where the adults in households make group decisions about sharing costs or resources, a household-based analysis may be preferred to a person-level analysis. And a government may wish to know what share of its housing is owner-occupied for policy considerations, such as taxation purposes.

But the HPOP provides a homeownership measure that is better-suited for many policy conversations. For example, land use regulations are getting increased attention from policymakers and the press. The HPOP allows us to consider the potential relationship between the built environment and people’s preferences for living with other adults. When people live in an area where the housing stock can accommodate multi-generational households, do we see more multi-generational households?

The HPOP provides a homeownership measure that is better-suited for many policy conversations.

The new measure could also allow researchers to consider the long-term financial implications for young adults who spend more time living in the households of their homeowner parents. Compared to young adults who move out sooner, are these young adults more likely to become homeowners, or do they invest in education or housing differently as they age?

The HPOP enables such explorations by describing the homeownership rate for all adults. In providing a person-level statistic, the HPOP avoids lumping together all the adults in a household as either being renters or owners. It identifies a new housing status for adults who aren’t fully captured by the standard designations of “renter” or “homeowner.” Many of these adults likely are paying some kind of rent but are nevertheless excluded from standard renter counts.

Our estimates of the HPOP are available for you to explore. We look forward to seeing what others might discover through this more nuanced perspective on the nation’s homeowners.

Endnotes

1 It is also possible to compute the HPOP from many other datasets, such as the American Housing Survey (AHS) and the Current Population Survey (CPS). The ACS and the CPS are products of the U.S. Census Bureau, while the AHS is sponsored by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development and conducted by the U.S. Census Bureau.

2 To access more detail about this process, see our Homeowners-to-Population Data page.

3 Based on 2024 ACS public-use microdata.

blocks.html_en: To further its mission of pursuing a growing economy and stable financial system that work for all of us, the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis works to understand economic conditions in the Ninth Federal Reserve District and beyond. Homeownership trends are a critical component of those conditions. Housing is both the largest expense and the largest source of wealth for many families. Using the HPOP helps us understand housing better in several ways. For example, the HPOP accounts for millions of American adults who would not be identified as either homeowners or renters under the traditional owner-occupancy measure. By including all adults, the HPOP gives us a more accurate understanding of the economic well-being of Americans. Measuring homeownership by the person instead of by the home is particularly important for comparing characteristics of homeowners: by age, by geography, and across time.

blocks.html_en: Calculating the HPOP

sentences.en: Calculating the HPOP
sentences.zh: 计算 HPOP

blocks.html_en: To calculate the HPOP, we divide the total population of adult homeowners by the total adult population. For this analysis, we use data from the U.S. Census Bureau’s American Community Survey (ACS).1 To determine the total population of adults, we exclude anyone under age 18. To identify homeowners, we then look among the adults living in owner-occupied housing. The ACS tags the homeowner within such homes as the “reference person” (or “head”) for their household. We then also include any spouses or unmarried partners of the reference person as homeowners.2

sentences.en: To calculate the HPOP, we divide the total population of adult homeowners by the total adult population.
sentences.zh: 为了计算 HPOP，我们将成年房主总人口除以总成年人口。

sentences.en: For this analysis, we use data from the U.S. Census Bureau’s American Community Survey (ACS).1 To determine the total population of adults, we exclude anyone under age 18.
sentences.zh: 在本分析中，我们使用美国人口普查局美国社区调查 (ACS) 的数据。1 为了确定成年人口总数，我们排除了 18 岁以下的任何人。

sentences.en: To identify homeowners, we then look among the adults living in owner-occupied housing.
sentences.zh: 为了确定房主，我们会在居住在自住住房中的成年人中进行调查。

sentences.en: The ACS tags the homeowner within such homes as the “reference person” (or “head”) for their household.
sentences.zh: ACS 将此类房屋中的房主标记为其家庭的“参考人”（或“户主”）。

sentences.en: We then also include any spouses or unmarried partners of the reference person as homeowners.2
sentences.zh: 然后，我们还将参考人的任何配偶或未婚伴侣视为房主。2

blocks.html_en: The HPOP categorizes adults who are not homeowners more precisely than the owner-occupancy rate. The largest group of non-homeowners, renters, is accounted for in both measures. However, calculations of the owner-occupancy rate leave out people who are living in group quarters—such as college dormitories, nursing homes, and correctional facilities. Our calculations of the HPOP include those groups and also account for discrepancies in the number of adults in renter households relative to homeowner households. For instance, if 50 out of 100 homes were rented by 50 adults living alone and the other 50 homes were owned and occupied by 100 adults living as married couples, then the owner-occupancy rate would be 50 percent but the HPOP would be 67 percent.

sentences.en: The HPOP categorizes adults who are not homeowners more precisely than the owner-occupancy rate.
sentences.zh: HPOP 对非房主成年人的分类比房主入住率更准确。

sentences.en: The largest group of non-homeowners, renters, is accounted for in both measures.

sentences.en: However, calculations of the owner-occupancy rate leave out people who are living in group quarters—such as college dormitories, nursing homes, and correctional facilities.
sentences.zh: 然而，业主入住率的计算忽略了住在集体宿舍的人，例如大学宿舍、疗养院和惩教设施。

sentences.en: Our calculations of the HPOP include those groups and also account for discrepancies in the number of adults in renter households relative to homeowner households.
sentences.zh: 我们对 HPOP 的计算包括这些群体，并且还考虑了租房家庭相对于房主家庭的成年人数量差异。

sentences.en: For instance, if 50 out of 100 homes were rented by 50 adults living alone and the other 50 homes were owned and occupied by 100 adults living as married couples, then the owner-occupancy rate would be 50 percent but the HPOP would be 67 percent.
sentences.zh: 例如，如果 100 套住房中的 50 套由 50 名独居成年人出租，另外 50 套住房由 100 名已婚成年人拥有和占用，那么业主入住率为 50%，但 HPOP 将为 67%。

blocks.html_en: Our approach also quantifies that 13.9 percent of adults in the United States live in owner-occupied homes but are not owners themselves. In other words, more than one in eight of the nation’s adults are misrepresented in the most-cited statistic on homeownership. As Figure 2 indicates, these adults include adult children living with their homeowning parents; older parents living with their homeowning adult children; other relatives, such as siblings or cousins; and other unrelated adults, such as friends or roommates.

sentences.en: Our approach also quantifies that 13.9 percent of adults in the United States live in owner-occupied homes but are not owners themselves.
sentences.zh: 我们的方法还量化了美国 13.9% 的成年人居住在业主自住的房屋中，但他们自己并不是业主。

blocks.html_en: New measure highlights reductions in numbers of young homeowners

sentences.en: New measure highlights reductions in numbers of young homeowners

blocks.html_en: Policymakers are often interested in homeownership rates for specific population groups. Homeownership’s role in wealth building and financial independence has led to interest in the Gen Z and Millennial generations’ homeownership rates. For example, one recent survey garnered a lot of attention when it put the median age of a first-time home buyer at 40 years old (though several other data sources suggest the median first-time home buyer’s age is actually in the early 30s).

blocks.html_en: The HPOP categorizes adults who are not homeowners more precisely than the owner-occupancy rate.

sentences.en: The HPOP categorizes adults who are not homeowners more precisely than the owner-occupancy rate.
sentences.zh: HPOP 对非房主成年人的分类比房主入住率更准确。

blocks.html_en: Since the owner-occupancy rate is defined at the housing-unit level, descriptions of owner-occupancy rates by demographic characteristics, such as age, race, or sex, can be misleading. In the owner-occupancy approach, one homeowner or renter is defined as the head of a household. That person’s demographic characteristics are then used to represent all the residents. An advantage of the HPOP is that since it’s defined using all the adult residents, it can more accurately describe homeownership by demographic characteristic. These differences become clear when we look at homeownership rates by age.

sentences.en: These differences become clear when we look at homeownership rates by age.
sentences.zh: 当我们按年龄查看住房拥有率时，这些差异就变得很明显。

blocks.html_en: Across both the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP, young people have low homeownership rates. Even so, the owner-occupancy rate inflates homeownership among young adults, in two ways. First, as noted above, owner-occupancy rates by age are defined by the age of the household head. Because only a third of adults under age 35 are household heads,3 many young adults are not reflected in the rate calculation. This includes students or young workers who choose to reside at home with their parents until they’ve saved up some money or increased their earnings. Second, the owner-occupancy approach fails to account for anyone living in college dorms. They’re excluded from the calculation because dorms are categorized as group quarters.

sentences.en: Across both the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP, young people have low homeownership rates.
sentences.zh: 无论是自住率还是HPOP，年轻人的住房拥有率都较低。

sentences.en: Even so, the owner-occupancy rate inflates homeownership among young adults, in two ways.
sentences.zh: 即便如此，自住率仍然以两种方式抬高了年轻人的住房拥有率。

blocks.html_en: The owner-occupancy rate for households headed by adults under age 35 was 37 percent in 2024. Using the HPOP, which accounts for co-residents and dorm dwellers, we find a substantially lower number: only 22 percent of adults under 35 own their homes.

sentences.en: The owner-occupancy rate for households headed by adults under age 35 was 37 percent in 2024.
sentences.zh: 2024 年，以 35 岁以下成年人为户主的家庭自住率为 37%。

sentences.en: Using the HPOP, which accounts for co-residents and dorm dwellers, we find a substantially lower number: only 22 percent of adults under 35 own their homes.
sentences.zh: 使用 HPOP（包括同住者和宿舍居民），我们发现这个数字要低得多：只有 22% 的 35 岁以下成年人拥有自己的房屋。

blocks.html_en: Switching from the owner-occupancy rate to the HPOP also highlights how homeownership rates by age have evolved over time. Our HPOP approach indicates that the data from the past two decades have at least two stories to tell. First, young people’s homeownership rate fell and then rose. From 2006 until 2015, the HPOP among younger Americans dropped. For example, the HPOP for a 25-year-old fell from 20 percent in 2006 to 12 percent in 2015. It then increased from 2015 to 2024. As shown in Figure 3, in 2024 14 percent of 25-year-olds owned their homes. Second, the HPOP among adults over age 70 climbed upward during both periods, increasing by 5 percentage points in total. This could be the result of increased longevity and financial security among this group. In contrast, if we use owner-occupancy as our measure, we find only a 1 percentage point increase—a finding that largely misses the homeownership rate trend among older adults over this period. One reason the findings are different might be that many older adults would have either been co-residing with their children or living in nursing homes and thus not captured by the owner-occupancy measure.

sentences.en: One reason the findings are different might be that many older adults would have either been co-residing with their children or living in nursing homes and thus not captured by the owner-occupancy measure.

blocks.html_en: In addition to shedding light on differences in homeownership by demographics, the HPOP paints a significantly different picture of how homeownership varies among states. In doing so, it presents a stronger relationship between housing prices and homeownership than the owner-occupancy rate does. Our analysis shows that every state has a lower HPOP than owner-occupancy rate. In Figure 4, the y-axis values of the gold dots represent the differences between the two measures for all 50 states. We find that states with higher housing costs tend to have even lower HPOPs relative to their owner-occupancy rates. To measure housing prices, we use the rent-to-income ratio, which is the average annual rent divided by the average personal income among adult renters. Rent is generally a more consistent measure of housing prices, since the typical cost of owning a home can vary as mortgage rates and other homeownership costs shift over time. Comparing rents to incomes allows us to factor in state-by-state differences in typical wages. The higher the rent-to-income ratio, the higher a state’s housing costs.

sentences.zh: 除了揭示人口统计数据中住房拥有率的差异外，HPOP 还描绘了各州住房拥有率差异的显着不同的情况。

sentences.en: In doing so, it presents a stronger relationship between housing prices and homeownership than the owner-occupancy rate does.
sentences.zh: 由此可见，房价与住房拥有率之间的关系比业主入住率之间的关系更为密切。

sentences.en: Our analysis shows that every state has a lower HPOP than owner-occupancy rate.
sentences.zh: 我们的分析表明，每个州的 HPOP 都低于自住率。

sentences.en: In Figure 4, the y-axis values of the gold dots represent the differences between the two measures for all 50 states.
sentences.zh: 在图 4 中，金点的 y 轴值代表所有 50 个州的两种度量之间的差异。

sentences.en: We find that states with higher housing costs tend to have even lower HPOPs relative to their owner-occupancy rates.
sentences.zh: 我们发现，住房成本较高的州的 HPOP 相对于其自住率而言往往更低。

sentences.en: To measure housing prices, we use the rent-to-income ratio, which is the average annual rent divided by the average personal income among adult renters.
sentences.zh: 为了衡量房价，我们使用租金收入比，即成年租房者的平均年租金除以平均个人收入。

sentences.en: Rent is generally a more consistent measure of housing prices, since the typical cost of owning a home can vary as mortgage rates and other homeownership costs shift over time.
sentences.zh: 租金通常是衡量房价的更一致的指标，因为拥有房屋的典型成本可能会随着抵押贷款利率和其他住房拥有成本的变化而变化。

sentences.en: Comparing rents to incomes allows us to factor in state-by-state differences in typical wages.
sentences.zh: 通过比较租金和收入，我们可以考虑各州典型工资的差异。

sentences.en: The higher the rent-to-income ratio, the higher a state’s housing costs.
sentences.zh: 租金与收入之比越高，一个州的住房成本就越高。

blocks.html_en: Why would the difference between the two homeownership measures be so closely tied to housing costs? The most obvious explanation is that in places where housing is cheaper, more adults are buying their own homes. Of course, monthly expenses are only one factor people consider when they are deciding how—and with whom—they’d like to live. Housing situations might change when people get married. Some adults might want to take care of their aging parents or give their adult children a more affordable place to live. Some homeowners might prefer living with other people, leading them to rent out a spare room instead of using it as a guest room or an office.

sentences.en: Housing situations might change when people get married.
sentences.zh: 当人们结婚时，住房情况可能会发生变化。

sentences.zh: 一些成年人可能想照顾年迈的父母或为成年子女提供更实惠的居住地。

blocks.html_en: As higher housing costs motivate more adults to live together, the difference between the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP grows.

sentences.zh: 随着更高的住房成本促使更多的成年人住在一起，业主入住率和 HPOP 之间的差异越来越大。

blocks.html_en: Personal preferences influence some of these decisions. But economic conditions, such as the price of housing, are another influence. For example, the more expensive it is to rent or own a home, the more appealing it is to split those costs with others. As higher housing costs motivate more adults to live together, the difference between the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP grows.

blocks.html_en: To continue our examination of housing costs and homeownership, consider the implications for two states: Hawaii and North Dakota. Hawaii, which appears as the topmost gold circle back in Figure 4, is nearly the least affordable state, as measured by its rent-to-income ratio of 0.57. Consistent with the idea that higher housing costs might discourage adults from forming their own households, Hawaii’s HPOP is 18.9 percentage points lower than its owner-occupancy rate, the largest difference of any state.

sentences.en: To continue our examination of housing costs and homeownership, consider the implications for two states: Hawaii and North Dakota.
sentences.zh: 为了继续我们对住房成本和住房拥有率的研究，请考虑这对两个州的影响：夏威夷和北达科他州。

sentences.en: Hawaii, which appears as the topmost gold circle back in Figure 4, is nearly the least affordable state, as measured by its rent-to-income ratio of 0.57.
sentences.zh: 夏威夷在图 4 中显示为最上面的黄金圈，按其 0.57 的租金与收入比率衡量，它几乎是负担能力最差的州。

sentences.en: Consistent with the idea that higher housing costs might discourage adults from forming their own households, Hawaii’s HPOP is 18.9 percentage points lower than its owner-occupancy rate, the largest difference of any state.
sentences.zh: 与较高的住房成本可能会阻碍成年人组建自己的家庭的想法相一致，夏威夷的 HPOP 比自住率低 18.9 个百分点，是所有州中差异最大的。

blocks.html_en: On the other hand, North Dakota—one of four states that lie wholly within the Ninth Federal Reserve District, which the Minneapolis Fed serves—is the most affordable state in the nation by rent-to-income ratio. As we might expect, it has the smallest difference between its owner-occupancy rate and its HPOP. This small difference reflects, for example, how few adults under age 35 co-reside with parents in North Dakota. Only 10 percent of North Dakota’s young adults live with a parent, compared to the national average of 30 percent. While the gap between the two homeownership measures is more than 10 percentage points for the majority of states, in North Dakota the difference is just 3.9 percentage points. As a result, North Dakota’s homeownership ranking among the 50 states rises from forty-seventh to twenty-fourth when using the HPOP instead of the owner-occupancy rate.

sentences.en: On the other hand, North Dakota—one of four states that lie wholly within the Ninth Federal Reserve District, which the Minneapolis Fed serves—is the most affordable state in the nation by rent-to-income ratio.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，北达科他州是完全位于明尼阿波利斯联储服务的第九联邦储备区的四个州之一，按租金与收入比率计算，是全国最负担得起的州。

sentences.zh: 北达科他州只有 10% 的年轻人与父母同住，而全国平均水平为 30%。

sentences.en: As a result, North Dakota’s homeownership ranking among the 50 states rises from forty-seventh to twenty-fourth when using the HPOP instead of the owner-occupancy rate.
sentences.zh: 结果，当使用 HPOP 代替业主入住率时，北达科他州的住房拥有率在 50 个州中的排名从第 47 位上升到第 24 位。

blocks.html_en: Minnesota, South Dakota, and Montana—the other three states that lie wholly within the Ninth District—are relatively affordable places, as measured by rent-to-income ratio, and their homeownership rankings look more like North Dakota’s than Hawaii’s. As we might expect based on the relationship between housing costs and homeownership-measure gaps, their HPOPs are modestly lower than their owner-occupancy rates, with differences of 8.9, 6.0, and 10.1 percentage points, respectively, compared with a nationwide difference of 12.2 percentage points. Despite having higher housing costs, Montana ranks eighteenth in both the owner-occupancy rate and the HPOP. Figure 5 shows that both Minnesota and South Dakota improve their homeownership rank when we use the HPOP as the measure, moving from eighth and twentieth place, respectively, to fifth and seventh place.

blocks.html_en: Implications for public policy

sentences.en: Implications for public policy
sentences.zh: 对公共政策的影响

blocks.html_en: Using the owner-occupancy rate as a measure of homeownership can still serve an important purpose. For example, in cases where the adults in households make group decisions about sharing costs or resources, a household-based analysis may be preferred to a person-level analysis. And a government may wish to know what share of its housing is owner-occupied for policy considerations, such as taxation purposes.

sentences.en: Using the owner-occupancy rate as a measure of homeownership can still serve an important purpose.
sentences.zh: 使用自住率作为衡量住房拥有率的指标仍然具有重要作用。

sentences.en: For example, in cases where the adults in households make group decisions about sharing costs or resources, a household-based analysis may be preferred to a person-level analysis.
sentences.zh: 例如，如果家庭中的成年人就分担成本或资源做出集体决策，则基于家庭的分析可能优于个人分析。

sentences.en: And a government may wish to know what share of its housing is owner-occupied for policy considerations, such as taxation purposes.
sentences.zh: 出于政策考虑（例如税收目的），政府可能希望知道其住房中有多少比例是业主自住的。

blocks.html_en: But the HPOP provides a homeownership measure that is better-suited for many policy conversations. For example, land use regulations are getting increased attention from policymakers and the press. The HPOP allows us to consider the potential relationship between the built environment and people’s preferences for living with other adults. When people live in an area where the housing stock can accommodate multi-generational households, do we see more multi-generational households?

blocks.html_en: The new measure could also allow researchers to consider the long-term financial implications for young adults who spend more time living in the households of their homeowner parents. Compared to young adults who move out sooner, are these young adults more likely to become homeowners, or do they invest in education or housing differently as they age?

sentences.en: The new measure could also allow researchers to consider the long-term financial implications for young adults who spend more time living in the households of their homeowner parents.
sentences.zh: 这项新措施还可以让研究人员考虑对花更多时间住在房主父母家里的年轻人的长期财务影响。

sentences.en: Compared to young adults who move out sooner, are these young adults more likely to become homeowners, or do they invest in education or housing differently as they age?
sentences.zh: 与较早搬出的年轻人相比，这些年轻人是否更有可能成为房主，或者随着年龄的增长，他们对教育或住房的投资是否有所不同？

blocks.html_en: The HPOP enables such explorations by describing the homeownership rate for all adults. In providing a person-level statistic, the HPOP avoids lumping together all the adults in a household as either being renters or owners. It identifies a new housing status for adults who aren’t fully captured by the standard designations of “renter” or “homeowner.” Many of these adults likely are paying some kind of rent but are nevertheless excluded from standard renter counts.

sentences.zh: HPOP 通过描述所有成年人的住房拥有率来实现此类探索。

content_html_zh:
据报道，美国的住房拥有率为 65%。 但这个经常被引用的住房拥有率数据实际上是业主入住率，它告诉我们一个业主占用了多少套住房。 虽然业主入住率是一个有趣的指标，但它并不能告诉我们有多少人拥有自己的房子。 作为更好地反映成年人拥有房主比例的替代方案，我们提供了房主与人口比率（HPOP），这一衡量标准可以为重要的政策考虑因素和背景提供更细致的视角。 按照这一新衡量标准，美国的住房拥有率为 53%。

为了进一步实现为我们所有人服务的经济增长和金融体系稳定的使命，明尼阿波利斯联邦储备银行致力于了解第九联邦储备区及其他地区的经济状况。 住房拥有率趋势是这些条件的一个关键组成部分。 对于许多家庭来说，住房既是最大的支出，也是最大的财富来源。 使用 HPOP 可以从多个方面帮助我们更好地了解住房。 例如，HPOP 代表了数百万美国成年人，根据传统的业主自住衡量标准，他们既不会被认定为房主，也不会被认定为租房者。 通过将所有成年人纳入其中，HPOP 让我们能够更准确地了解美国人的经济福祉。 按个人而不是按房屋来衡量住房拥有率对于比较房主的特征（按年龄、按地理位置和跨时间）尤为重要。

了解住房拥有率措施

为了更好地理解传统业主自住措施和 HPOP 之间的区别，请考虑一个假设的有五个住房单元的死胡同，每个单元都有一个单独的居民群体：

住房单元 1. 一对夫妇拥有自己的房子。 该女子的父母与他们住在一起。 住房单元2。 一对夫妇拥有自己的房子。 他们的儿子刚刚大学毕业，和他们住在一起。 住房单元 3. 一个人拥有他的家。 一个朋友和他住在一起。 住房单元 4. 一对夫妇拥有自己的房子。 他们的两个年幼的孩子和他们住在一起。 住房单元 5. 三个室友租了他们的房子。 业主住在其他地方。

如图 1 所示，这 5 个住房单元住着 14 名成年人。 由于这 5 套住房中的 4 套都有其各自的业主作为居民，因此死胡同的业主入住率（传统上被视为房屋拥有率的衡量标准）为 80%。 然而，由于 14 名成年人中只有 7 名实际上是他们所居住房屋的所有者，因此 HPOP 要低得多，为 50%。

计算 HPOP

为了计算 HPOP，我们将成年房主总人口除以总成年人口。 在本分析中，我们使用美国人口普查局美国社区调查 (ACS) 的数据。1 为了确定成年人口总数，我们排除了 18 岁以下的任何人。 为了确定房主，我们会在居住在自住住房中的成年人中进行调查。 ACS 将此类房屋中的房主标记为其家庭的“参考人”（或“户主”）。 然后，我们还将参考人的任何配偶或未婚伴侣视为房主。2

HPOP 对非房主成年人的分类比房主入住率更准确。 这两项指标均涵盖了最大的非房主群体——租房者。 然而，业主入住率的计算忽略了住在集体宿舍的人，例如大学宿舍、疗养院和惩教设施。 我们对 HPOP 的计算包括这些群体，并且还考虑了租房家庭相对于房主家庭的成年人数量差异。 例如，如果 100 套住房中的 50 套由 50 名独居成年人出租，另外 50 套住房由 100 名已婚成年人拥有和占用，那么业主入住率为 50%，但 HPOP 将为 67%。

我们的方法还量化了美国 13.9% 的成年人居住在业主自住的房屋中，但他们自己并不是业主。 换句话说，在被引用最多的住房拥有率统计数据中，超过八分之一的全国成年人被歪曲了。 如图 2 所示，这些成年人包括与拥有住房的父母住在一起的成年子女；年长的父母与拥有房产的成年子女住在一起；其他亲戚，例如兄弟姐妹或表兄弟姐妹；以及其他无关的成年人，例如朋友或室友。

新措施凸显年轻房主数量的减少

政策制定者通常对特定人群的住房拥有率感兴趣。 住房拥有率在财富积累和财务独立方面的作用引起了人们对 Z 世代和千禧一代住房拥有率的兴趣。 例如，最近的一项调查引起了广泛关注，该调查显示首次购房者的中位年龄为 40 岁（尽管其他几个数据来源表明首次购房者的中位年龄实际上在 30 岁出头）。

HPOP 对非房主成年人的分类比房主入住率更准确。

由于自住率是在住房单元层面定义的，因此按年龄、种族或性别等人口特征对自住率的描述可能会产生误导。 在业主自住法中，一名房主或租户被定义为户主。 然后使用该人的人口统计特征来代表所有居民。 HPOP 的一个优点是，由于它是使用所有成年居民来定义的，因此它可以更准确地按人口特征描述住房拥有率。 当我们按年龄查看住房拥有率时，这些差异就变得很明显。

无论是自住率还是HPOP，年轻人的住房拥有率都较低。 即便如此，自住率仍然以两种方式抬高了年轻人的住房拥有率。 首先，如上所述，按年龄划分的业主入住率是根据户主的年龄来定义的。 由于 35 岁以下成年人中只有三分之一是户主3，因此许多年轻人没有反映在比率计算中。 这包括选择与父母一起住在家里的学生或年轻工人，直到他们存了一些钱或增加收入。 其次，业主自住的方法没有考虑到住在大学宿舍的人。 他们被排除在计算之外，因为宿舍被归类为集体宿舍。

由于 HPOP 是使用所有成年居民来定义的，因此它可以更准确地按人口特征描述住房拥有率。

2024 年，以 35 岁以下成年人为户主的家庭自住率为 37%。 使用 HPOP（包括同住者和宿舍居民），我们发现这个数字要低得多：只有 22% 的 35 岁以下成年人拥有自己的房屋。

从业主入住率转向 HPOP 也凸显了按年龄划分的住房拥有率如何随着时间的推移而演变。 我们的 HPOP 方法表明，过去二十年的数据至少有两个故事可讲。 年轻人住房拥有率先降后升。 从 2006 年到 2015 年，美国年轻人的 HPOP 有所下降。 例如，25 岁​​的 HPOP 从 2006 年的 20% 下降到 2015 年的 12%。 然后从 2015 年到 2024 年有所增加。 如图 3 所示，到 2024 年，14% 的 25 岁年轻人拥有自己的房屋。 其次，70岁以上成年人的HPOP在这两个时期均呈上升趋势，总共增加了5个百分点。 这可能是该群体寿命延长和财务安全的结果。 相比之下，如果我们使用自住率作为衡量标准，我们发现仅增加了 1 个百分点，这一发现在很大程度上忽略了这一时期老年人的住房拥有率趋势。 研究结果不同的原因之一可能是，许多老年人要么与孩子同住，要么住在疗养院，因此没有被纳入自住指标中。

对州一级的影响

除了揭示人口统计数据中住房拥有率的差异外，HPOP 还描绘了各州住房拥有率差异的显着不同的情况。 由此可见，房价与住房拥有率之间的关系比业主入住率之间的关系更为密切。 我们的分析表明，每个州的 HPOP 都低于自住率。 在图 4 中，金点的 y 轴值代表所有 50 个州的两种度量之间的差异。 我们发现，住房成本较高的州的 HPOP 相对于其自住率而言往往更低。 为了衡量房价，我们使用租金收入比，即成年租房者的平均年租金除以平均个人收入。 租金通常是衡量房价的更一致的指标，因为拥有房屋的典型成本可能会随着抵押贷款利率和其他住房拥有成本的变化而变化。 通过比较租金和收入，我们可以考虑各州典型工资的差异。 租金与收入之比越高，一个州的住房成本就越高。

加载图表 4...

为什么这两种住房拥有率指标之间的差异与住房成本密切相关？ 最明显的解释是，在房价便宜的地方，更多的成年人购买自己的房子。 当然，每月开支只是人们在决定如何生活以及与谁生活时考虑的因素之一。 当人们结婚时，住房情况可能会发生变化。 一些成年人可能想照顾年迈的父母或为成年子女提供更实惠的居住地。 一些房主可能更喜欢与其他人住在一起，导致他们出租闲置的房间，而不是将其用作客房或办公室。

随着更高的住房成本促使更多的成年人住在一起，业主入住率和 HPOP 之间的差异越来越大。

个人偏好会影响其中一些决定。 但经济状况，例如房价，是另一个影响因素。 例如，租房或拥有房屋越贵，与他人分摊这些费用就越有吸引力。 随着更高的住房成本促使更多的成年人住在一起，业主入住率和 HPOP 之间的差异越来越大。

图 5 使用户可以准确地看到每个州的两种住房拥有率衡量标准之间的差异有多大。 它还显示了从业主入住率转向HPOP时发生的排名变化。

为了继续我们对住房成本和住房拥有率的研究，请考虑这对两个州的影响：夏威夷和北达科他州。 夏威夷在图 4 中显示为最上面的黄金圈，按其 0.57 的租金与收入比率衡量，它几乎是负担能力最差的州。 与较高的住房成本可能会阻碍成年人组建自己的家庭的想法相一致，夏威夷的 HPOP 比自住率低 18.9 个百分点，是所有州中差异最大的。

另一方面，北达科他州是完全位于明尼阿波利斯联储服务的第九联邦储备区的四个州之一，按租金与收入比率计算，是全国最负担得起的州。 正如我们所预料的，它的自住率和 HPOP 之间的差异最小。 例如，这种微小的差异反映出，北达科他州 35 岁以下的成年人与父母同住的人数很少。 北达科他州只有 10% 的年轻人与父母同住，而全国平均水平为 30%。 虽然大多数州这两种住房拥有率指标之间的差距超过 10 个百分点，但在北达科他州，这一差距仅为 3.9 个百分点。 结果，当使用 HPOP 代替业主入住率时，北达科他州的住房拥有率在 50 个州中的排名从第 47 位上升到第 24 位。

从租金收入比来看，明尼苏达州、南达科他州和蒙大拿州（完全位于第九区的另外三个州）是相对负担得起的地方，而且它们的住房拥有率排名看起来更像北达科他州，而不是夏威夷。 正如我们根据住房成本与住房拥有率差距之间的关系所预期的那样，他们的 HPOP 略低于自住率，差异分别为 8.9、6.0 和 10.1 个百分点，而全国范围内的差异为 12.2 个百分点。 尽管蒙大拿州的住房成本较高，但其业主入住率和 HPOP 均排名第 18。 图 5 显示，当我们使用 HPOP 作为衡量指标时，明尼苏达州和南达科他州的住房拥有率排名均有所提高，分别从第八位和第二十位上升至第五位和第七位。

对公共政策的影响

使用自住率作为衡量住房拥有率的指标仍然具有重要作用。 例如，如果家庭中的成年人就分担成本或资源做出集体决策，则基于家庭的分析可能优于个人分析。 出于政策考虑（例如税收目的），政府可能希望知道其住房中有多少比例是业主自住的。

但 HPOP 提供了一种更适合许多政策对话的住房拥有率衡量标准。 例如，土地使用法规越来越受到政策制定者和媒体的关注。 HPOP 让我们能够考虑建筑环境与人们与其他成年人一起居住的偏好之间的潜在关系。 当人们居住在一个住房存量可以容纳多代家庭的地区时，我们是否会看到更多的多代家庭？

HPOP 提供了一种更适合许多政策对话的住房拥有率衡量标准。

这项新措施还可以让研究人员考虑对花更多时间住在房主父母家里的年轻人的长期财务影响。 与较早搬出的年轻人相比，这些年轻人是否更有可能成为房主，或者随着年龄的增长，他们对教育或住房的投资是否有所不同？

HPOP 通过描述所有成年人的住房拥有率来实现此类探索。 在提供个人层面的统计数据时，HPOP 避免将家庭中的所有成年人归为租户或业主。 它为那些没有完全被“租客”或“房主”标准名称所涵盖的成年人确定了一种新的住房地位。 这些成年人中的许多人可能正在支付某种租金，但仍然被排除在标准租房者人数之外。

我们对 HPOP 的估计可供您探索。 我们期待看到其他人通过这个对国家房主更细致的视角可能会发现什么。

尾注

1 还可以根据许多其他数据集计算 HPOP，例如美国住房调查 (AHS) 和当前人口调查 (CPS)。 ACS 和 CPS 是美国人口普查局的产品，而 AHS 由美国住房和城市发展部赞助并由美国人口普查局执行。

2 要了解有关此过程的更多详细信息，请参阅我们的房主人口数据页面。
